chunks.text: Every year, about eight million tons of plastic flow into the world's ocean.
That equals one truckload every minute.
a huge area of garbage twice the size of Texas is floating
called the Great Pacific Garbage Patch.
Debris carried by the currents has gathered there over many years.
Larger plastic, broken down by waves and ultraviolet light, slowly becomes microplastic.
These tiny particles enter the food chain, from plankton to fish
and eventually onto our dinner tables.
The impact on marine life is severe.
Whales and seabirds confused plastic bags with their food and have died after swallowing them.
Coral reefs, too, face the double threat of chemical pollution and warming waters.
Governments around the world have introduced various policies
such as charging for plastic bags and the ban of certain plastics.
Companies, on the other hand, have invested in biodegradable materials and recycling technology.
In the end, the problem will be solved by the choice each of us makes.
The accumulation of small choices in daily life will change the future of the ocean.
